food_subgroup: Cooked prawn / shrimp dish with Indian spices

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists for Indian spiced prawn masala specifically; closest parent 'shrimp food product' used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CJW (Crustaceans, cooked) is the closest defensible parent; no specific Indian prawn masala code exists in FoodEx2.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT code identified for Indian spiced prawn masala dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One restaurant serving of Kinara Jhinga is approximately 250 g, typically comprising 8–10 medium-to-large prawns in a spiced masala sauce, served as a main course.

nutrient_source.title: Prawn curry (Indian-style), cooked — ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: ICMR-NIN values for cooked prawn curry (Indian-style) used as the primary reference. Kinara Jhinga is a restaurant-prepared Indian spiced prawn dish; macros adapted from ICMR-NIN prawn curry data. Values represent a composite of prawn flesh and masala sauce. Sodium adjusted upward to reflect restaurant-level seasoning typical of commercial Indian prawn dishes.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Mixed Seafood and Protein-Dominant Dishes — University of Sydney GI Database; analogous estimate from prawn/shrimp curry category
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Kinara Jhinga. GI estimated at 35 based on the University of Sydney GI Database classification of protein-dominant seafood dishes with low carbohydrate content (predominantly from onion, tomato, and spice paste). Prawn-based dishes with masala sauce are consistently low-GI due to high protein content and minimal starchy carbohydrate. GL per serving = (3.5 g carb per 100g × 250 g serving / 100) × 35 / 100 = 3.1.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from restaurant-level seasoning (~480 mg/100 g; ~1200 mg per serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should consume Kinara Jhinga in moderation; a single restaurant serving may contribute approximately 50% of the recommended daily sodium intake of 2000 mg, and portion control or requesting reduced salt preparation is advisable.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~35) and low carbohydrate content (~3.5 g/100 g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Kinara Jhinga is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to its low GI of approximately 35 and low glycemic load per serving (~3.1), making it unlikely to cause significant postprandial blood glucose elevation when consumed as part of a balanced meal.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated dietary cholesterol from prawns (~145 mg/100 g) and moderate sodium
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should be mindful of the cholesterol content from prawns (~362 mg per serving) and the moderate saturated fat level; however, the omega-3 fatty acid contribution from prawns and the low saturated fat relative to total fat may partially offset cardiovascular risk when consumed in moderation.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Kinara Jhinga is an Indian spiced prawn dish served at Kinara restaurant at Boat Quay, Singapore. 'Jhinga' is the Hindi word for prawn or shrimp, and this dish represents a classic North Indian-style prawn masala preparation. The dish features large, succulent prawns cooked in a richly spiced sauce made from onions, tomatoes, garlic, ginger, and a blend of aromatic spices including cumin, coriander, turmeric, chilli, and garam masala. The cooking method involves sautéing the spice base until fragrant, then cooking the prawns in the masala until they are just cooked through, resulting in a dish with a deep reddish-orange sauce that clings to the prawns. The texture is tender and juicy with a sauce that is thick and aromatic. The flavour profile is bold, savoury, mildly spicy, and complex, with the natural sweetness of fresh prawns complementing the earthy spice blend. Nutritionally, Kinara Jhinga is a high-protein, moderate-fat, low-carbohydrate dish. Per 100 g it provides approximately 118 kcal, 14.2 g protein, 5.8 g fat, and only 3.5 g carbohydrate, making it a protein-dense option. The glycemic index is estimated at approximately 35 (low), making it suitable for individuals managing blood glucose. The dish is naturally gluten-free and dairy-free. The primary nutritional concern is sodium, which is elevated at restaurant-preparation levels (~480 mg/100 g), relevant for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. Prawns are a good source of vitamin B12, phosphorus, selenium, and iodine. This dish is typically served as a main course alongside naan, roti, or rice at Indian restaurants in Singapore.

confidence_reason: Kinara Jhinga is a restaurant-specific dish with no direct entry in ICMR-NIN or HPB Singapore Foodo databases. Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN prawn curry (Indian-style) data, which is the closest approved primary source. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database category for protein-dominant seafood dishes. Confidence is medium rather than high because no exact dish-level measurement exists in an approved primary source.
notes: Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN prawn curry source data. Vitamin E (vitamin_e_mg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN prawn curry source data. Selenium (selenium_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN prawn curry source data. Trans fat (trans_fat_g) is 0.0; confirmed zero as prawns and vegetable oil-based masala preparations contain negligible trans fat per ICMR-NIN data. Monounsaturated and polyunsaturated fat values are estimated from ICMR-NIN fatty acid profiles for cooked prawn dishes. Sodium is adjusted to 480 mg/100 g to reflect typical restaurant-level seasoning; ICMR-NIN home-cooked reference value is lower (~280 mg/100 g). GI of 35 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for this specific dish exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GL per serving calculated as: (3.5 g/100 g × 250 g) / 100 × 35 = 3.06, rounded to 3.1. Food is classified as Indian cuisine served in Singapore; multilingual names follow Singaporean dish rules (English, Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi). The dish is described as 'In House Prawn Special' on field survey, confirming it is a signature prawn preparation at Kinara - Boat Quay.
